notable.notable_blurb: Donated Virgil collection to Princeton; amateur photographer

narrative_blocks.header: His wife died at 29; he never remarried
narrative_blocks.body: He married Elise de Rham in April 1876; their only child died in infancy and Elise herself died in October 1879 at age 29. He never remarried and lived out his life at the family property on Brooklyn Heights.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent Virgil editions to Princeton every year
narrative_blocks.body: As a student he began collecting early editions of the Latin poet Virgil and donated the collection to Princeton University Library in 1896, adding new volumes every year until his death in 1923. He was also a major donor of European prints and drawings to what is now the Princeton University Art Museum.

narrative_blocks.body: He married Elise de Rham in April 1876; their only child died in infancy and Elise herself died in October 1879 at age 29. He never remarried and lived out his life at the family property on Brooklyn Heights.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

cross_connections.display_label: Henry Evelyn Pierrepont co-founded the American Geographical Society (1851) alongside.

narrative_blocks.header: Designated the land that became Green-Wood
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed in 1833 to lay out the streets of Brooklyn, he set aside the glacial moraine above Gowanus Creek for what would become America's most influential rural cemetery. He co-founded Green-Wood and served as its president from 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: His Brooklyn street plan included eleven parks
narrative_blocks.body: He drew the grid of the newly incorporated City of Brooklyn while traveling in Europe in 1833. Some historians call him one of the first city planners in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: Monopolized East River ferries until the bridge
narrative_blocks.body: In 1844 he merged five operators into the Brooklyn Union Ferry Company. The monopoly held for nearly forty years until the Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883.

narrative_blocks.header: Owned a Stuart Washington portrait Lafayette authenticated
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather William Constable commissioned the painting from Gilbert Stuart in 1797 as a duplicate of the Lansdowne. Lafayette saw it in 1824 and said 'That is really my old friend indeed.'

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded the American Geographical Society in 1851
narrative_blocks.body: He served on its founding council for ten years and was first president of the Brooklyn Academy of Music. Pierrepont Street in Brooklyn Heights still carries the family name.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Rutherfurd Clarkson (daughter of Gen. Matthew Clarkson)

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the Grace Church Employment Society in 1865
narrative_blocks.body: She founded the Grace Church Employment Society in Brooklyn in 1865, providing work referrals to women in need. Her daughter Anna Jay Pierrepont led the organization for decades after her death in 1902.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her father Peter Augustus Jay built this Greek Revival mansion in 1838.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

narrative_blocks.header: Aged Brooklyn gin a full year for mellowness
narrative_blocks.body: In 1801 he bought Philip Livingston's old brewery on the Brooklyn waterfront and converted it to the Anchor Gin Distillery. The site closed in 1819 and is now part of One Brooklyn Bridge Park.

narrative_blocks.header: Half a million acres at eight cents apiece
narrative_blocks.body: His 1802 marriage to Anna Maria Constable made him heir to her father William Constable's Northern New York holdings. The land had been bought decades earlier when there was no buyer for it.
He sketched a public walkway along the bluffs above the harbor more than a century before it was built. The Promenade as it exists today follows the route he proposed.

narrative_blocks.header: Pirates seized his ship and bankrupted him
narrative_blocks.body: The Confederacy was taken at sea in 1797 carrying his firm's cargo. The loss ended his Paris-based mercantile career and forced him home to start again.

narrative_blocks.header: Witnessed Robespierre's execution from the crowd
narrative_blocks.body: He spent seven years in France during the 1790s tending his firm's shipments. He was in Paris for the Reign of Terror and saw the head of the Jacobin regime guillotined.

notable.notable_blurb: Argued Black suffrage at NY Convention, 1821

parents.father_name: John Jay (1745-1829), first Chief Justice of the United States

career.organization: Chief Justice John Jay's London Mission (Jay Treaty)
career.notes: Served as private secretary to his father during negotiation of the Jay Treaty with Britain in London.
Argued for Black suffrage during the convention; motion was overruled.
Society founded by his father John Jay and Alexander Hamilton in 1785.
Co-founded with Thomas Eddy — earliest savings bank in New York.
Oldest law library in New York City.

narrative_blocks.header: Argued Black suffrage at 1821 NY Convention
narrative_blocks.body: At the 1821 New York State Constitutional Convention he moved that Black men be granted the same suffrage as white men — arguing that 'the idea that black people were naturally inferior had long been completely refuted and universally exploded.' The motion was overruled, and New York imposed a $250 property requirement on Black voters that effectively disenfranchised most. He was forty-five and had just stepped down as Recorder of New York City.

narrative_blocks.header: Led the Manumission Society his father co-founded
narrative_blocks.body: He became President of the New-York Manumission Society in 1816 — the abolitionist society founded by his father John Jay and Alexander Hamilton in 1785. The role made him the institutional successor to the founding-era anti-slavery movement in New York. He also served simultaneously as President of the New York Public School Society, which his Manumission Society had helped create.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded New York Bank for Savings, 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Co-founded the New York Bank for Savings with Thomas Eddy in 1816 — the earliest savings bank in New York and one of the first in the United States. He later founded the New York Law Institute in 1828, today the oldest law library in New York City. Both institutions still operate.
